name: Colombian Andean Coffee Belt

staple_foods.name: Arabica coffee

key_flavor_bioactives.caffeine_concentration: 9.0-11.5 mg/g dry bean [Colombian washed Arabica — Mussatto et al. 2011 Trends Food Sci 22:404; Huila/Nariño mid-altitude]
key_flavor_bioactives.polyphenol_content: 3800-5400 mg/100g CGA equivalents [Colombian washed process — Campa et al. 2012 J Agric Food Chem 60:3252; slightly lower than Ethiopian at equivalent altitude due to processing]

terpenes.linalool: 0.5-1.2 mg/L in brewed coffee [Colombian washed — Buffo & Cardelli-Freire 2004 Flavour Fragrance J 19:99; lower than Ethiopian natural process]

organic_acids.malic_acid: 0.6-1.0 g/100g dry bean [Colombian mid-altitude — Lingle 2011 SCAA; intermediate between Ethiopian highland and Vietnamese lowland]

staple_foods.notes: Colombian coffee belt mid-altitude point for the 3-RSU altitude natural experiment. Colombian Castillo/Caturra cultivars are genetically distinct from Ethiopian landraces — this introduces cultivar noise but the altitude signal is still large enough to detect. CGA and malic acid intermediate between Ethiopian highland (RSU-17, 2200m) and Vietnamese lowland (RSU-19, 700m). Washed processing removes mucilage, reducing linalool relative to Ethiopian natural process. Colombian volcanic soils (Andosols) drain rapidly and supply mineral nutrients differently from Ethiopian basalt.
data_sources: Mussatto et al. (2011) — Coffee chemistry review, Trends Food Sci 22:404
Campa et al. (2012) — CGA across coffee origins, J Agric Food Chem 60:3252
Buffo & Cardelli-Freire (2004) — Coffee flavor overview, Flavour Fragrance J 19:99

staple_foods.name: arepa de choclo

primary_metabolites.protein_content: 3.5-5.0 g/100g [fresh corn arepa — estimate from nixtamal literature]

key_flavor_bioactives.polyphenol_content: 80-140 mg GAE/100g [yellow corn phenolics — Adom & Liu 2002 J Agric Food Chem 50:6182]

staple_foods.notes: Fresh corn (maize) cakes are the foundational carbohydrate in Colombian lowland-to-highland transition zones. Unlike Mexican nixtamal (which uses lime-alkali treatment), Colombian arepas use fresh or dried ground corn without alkaline processing. Polyphenol content of corn kernel varies with pigmentation.
data_sources: Adom & Liu (2002) — Antioxidant activity of grains, J Agric Food Chem 50:6182

staple_foods.name: papa criolla

organic_acids.citric_acid: 0.5-0.8 g/100g [Solanum phureja — Colombian yellow creole potato; Rodríguez et al. 2010 J Food Comp Anal 23:376]

umami_compounds.glutamate: 0.05-0.15 g/100g free glutamate [Solanum phureja — lower glutamate than wild Andean types; Rodríguez et al. 2010]

staple_foods.notes: Papa criolla (Solanum phureja) is Colombia's native diploid potato — small, yellow-fleshed, buttery, and fast-cooking. Grown at 1000-2800m in the Andes. This is a different Solanum species from RSU-10 (Andean S. andigenum) but still within the Andean potato clade — organic acid comparison between RSU-10 and RSU-18 is possible with cultivar annotation.
data_sources: Rodríguez et al. (2010) — Nutritional composition of papa criolla, J Food Comp Anal 23:376

staple_foods.name: chontaduro

primary_metabolites.protein_content: 3.4-5.0 g/100g [Bactris gasipaes — Yuyama et al. 2003 Food Chem 83:79]

key_flavor_bioactives.polyphenol_content: 120-200 mg GAE/100g [peach palm carotenoids and phenolics — Pacheco et al. 2008 Grasas y Aceites 59:45]

staple_foods.notes: Chontaduro (peach palm fruit) is the culturally distinctive fat/energy food of Pacific Colombian and Andean transition zones. Boiled and eaten with salt or honey, it is starchy, fatty, and nutritionally dense. High in beta-carotene (pro-vitamin A). Represents Amazonian/Andean palm food culture distinct from RSU-09.
data_sources: Yuyama et al. (2003) — Chemical composition of peach palm, Food Chem 83:79
Pacheco et al. (2008) — Peach palm lipid fractions, Grasas y Aceites 59:45

staple_foods.name: chicha de maiz

organic_acids.lactic_acid: 0.5-1.0 g/100g [traditional chicha — Medina & Rolz 1980 Appl Microbiol 40:150]

staple_foods.notes: Fermented corn beverage analogous to RSU-10 chicha de jora but at mid-altitude Colombia. Traditional preparation varies: some use salivary amylase inoculation (chewing), others use malted corn. The microbial community and resulting acid profile differ from the Peruvian highland version. Connects to RSU-10 for fermentation chemistry comparison.
data_sources: Medina & Rolz (1980) — Fermentation characteristics of chicha, Appl Microbiol 40:150

notes: Colombian Andean coffee belt (Eje Cafetero, Huila/Nariño region, ~1600m). Volcanic Andosol parent material, well-drained, high organic matter. Mid-altitude anchor for the 3-RSU coffee natural experiment (RSU-17 Ethiopia 2200m, RSU-18 Colombia 1600m, RSU-19 Vietnam 700m). Colombian washed processing introduces a processing variable on top of altitude — the clean cup of Colombian coffee vs Ethiopian natural process isolates fermentation effects. Note: arepa de choclo and papa criolla both present in carb slot; papa criolla listed as second carb for natural experiment continuity with RSU-10.
